north_indian.overview: North Indian cuisine is built on dairy (ghee, paneer, cream, yogurt), allium (onion, garlic in nearly every dish), and wheat (roti, naan, paratha). Nut-based gravies are common in Mughlai dishes. Spice blends like garam masala are standard.
threat_landscape: Ghee (clarified butter) is dairy — staff often believe it is not. Present in nearly every dish.
Onion and garlic are foundational to most gravies and curries. Even 'plain' dal typically contains both.
Cashew and almond pastes thicken many gravies (korma, butter chicken, malai dishes) without being listed.
Wheat flour is used in all breads (roti, naan, paratha, puri) and as a thickener in some gravies.
Yogurt (raita, marinades, kadhi) is pervasive — even tandoori meats are marinated in yogurt.
Sesame seeds appear in some breads and chutneys.
cross_contamination: Tandoor ovens cook naan, roti, and meats in shared space — wheat and dairy cross-contact is near-certain.
Shared oil for deep frying: samosas, pakoras, puris all fried in the same oil.
Street food vendors use single prep surfaces for all items.
Nut-based and dairy-based gravies prepared in the same vessels.
safe_alternatives: Jain preparation: no onion, no garlic, no root vegetables. Widely understood in India.
Sattvic cooking (ashram-style): avoids onion, garlic, and often avoids heavy spices.
South Indian dosa/idli if avoiding wheat (rice and lentil based) — but confirm no wheat flour added.
Plain steamed rice with dal (confirm no ghee tempering if dairy-free).

japanese.overview: Japanese cuisine relies heavily on soy sauce (contains wheat), dashi (fish stock in almost everything), and seafood. Buckwheat (soba) and wheat (udon, tempura) are staples. Egg appears unexpectedly in many dishes.
threat_landscape: Soy sauce contains wheat — it is in nearly every savory dish. Tamari is the wheat-free alternative but rare in restaurants.
Dashi (fish stock from bonito flakes + kombu) is the base of miso soup, noodle broths, simmered dishes, and many sauces. Even vegetable dishes often contain dashi.
Buckwheat (soba noodles) — severe allergen for some, often processed alongside wheat.
Egg is in tamagoyaki, oyakodon, ramen (ajitsuke tamago), tempura batter, and many sauces.
Shellfish: shrimp tempura, ebi, crab (kani), and shellfish-derived seasonings.
Sesame oil and sesame seeds are extremely common in dressings, toppings, and cooking.
cross_contamination: Tempura oil is shared across all items — shrimp, vegetables, fish all fried together. Wheat, egg, shellfish cross-contact.
Soba and udon often boiled in the same water. Buckwheat-wheat cross-contact is common.
Izakaya shared prep surfaces — sashimi and tempura cut on same boards.
Conveyor belt sushi: all items pass through the same environment.
safe_alternatives: Onigiri (rice balls) with simple fillings — confirm no soy sauce seasoning.
Plain steamed rice (gohan) is reliably allergen-free.
Edamame (soybeans) — if soy is not an issue, these are simple and safe.
Request 'shoyu-nuki' (no soy sauce) — but cross-contact from prep surfaces remains.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Cashew paste is rarely listed on menus but is standard. Yogurt in the marinade is invisible in final dish. Fenugreek can cause reactions in legume-sensitive individuals.
north_indian.alternatives: Jain version removes onion/garlic but retains dairy and nuts. Dairy-free version is not standard.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Ghee is often assumed to be 'not dairy' by staff. Some versions add cashew paste for creaminess.
north_indian.alternatives: Jain preparation removes onion/garlic but retains all dairy. Tofu substitution for paneer is non-standard in India.

north_indian.hidden_risks: The 'makhani' (butter) in the name signals dairy. Even 'plain dal' at most restaurants has ghee tempering (tadka). Some versions simmer overnight with butter.
north_indian.alternatives: Ask for dal without tadka/tempering — this removes ghee. Not standard.

milk.source: yogurt in dough, ghee brushed on surface

north_indian.hidden_risks: Even 'plain' naan contains yogurt in the dough and ghee on the surface. Garlic naan adds allium. Tandoor is shared with dairy-containing items.
north_indian.alternatives: Rice is the wheat-free alternative. No safe naan variant exists for wheat/gluten.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Generally dairy-free and nut-free. However, some restaurants add cream for richness. Served with bhatura (deep-fried wheat bread) by default — wheat cross-contact on the plate.
north_indian.alternatives: One of the safer North Indian options for dairy/nut-free diets. Jain version removes onion/garlic. Serve with rice instead of bhatura for wheat-free.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Fried onions are a primary flavor — impossible to remove. Some biryanis contain nuts (cashews, almonds) as garnish. Yogurt is in the marinade. Hyderabadi style may have boiled eggs.
north_indian.alternatives: Plain pulao is simpler but still contains ghee and onion. Steamed rice is the safe fallback.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Deep-fried in shared oil with other wheat and dairy items. Some fillings include paneer (dairy). Oil is typically reused extensively.
north_indian.alternatives: No safe alternative for wheat-free. Jain samosas exist (no onion/garlic) but still wheat.

north_indian.hidden_risks: The yogurt marinade is invisible in the final product. Red color may come from food coloring. Cooked in tandoor with naan — wheat cross-contact.
north_indian.alternatives: Reshmi kebab and seekh kebab also use yogurt. Difficult to find yogurt-free tandoor meats in North India.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Nut pastes are the DEFINING ingredient of korma — cannot be removed. This is one of the highest-risk dishes for nut allergies in Indian cuisine.
north_indian.alternatives: No safe alternative for nut-allergic diners. Choose a tomato-based curry (like rogan josh or vindaloo) instead.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Kashmiri versions may not use onion/garlic (Pandit-style) — but restaurant versions almost always do. Generally nut-free, making it safer than korma for nut allergies.
north_indian.alternatives: One of the better options for nut-free diets. Pandit-style version is allium-free.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Deep-fried in shared oil. Simpler than naan (no dairy in dough), but cross-contamination from shared fryer.
north_indian.alternatives: Rice or rice-based items for wheat-free.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Generally dairy-free and nut-free, making it one of the safer options. Some restaurants add cream or ghee for richness.
north_indian.alternatives: Jain version without onion/garlic. Confirm no ghee/cream added.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Khoya IS milk. Fried in ghee. Sugar syrup is the only safe component for dairy/wheat-allergic.
north_indian.alternatives: Fruit-based desserts or rice kheer (also dairy-based). Very few dairy-free Indian desserts exist.

north_indian.hidden_risks: This IS dairy. Often served automatically as a side. Cross-contact with main dishes if on the same plate.
north_indian.alternatives: Salad with lemon dressing as a cooling side instead.

north_indian.hidden_risks: The kofta balls contain cashew AND paneer. The gravy contains cream AND cashew paste. Triple dairy + nut exposure.
north_indian.alternatives: No safe variant for dairy or nut allergies. Choose tomato-based or lentil-based dishes.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Generally one of the safest options — no dairy or nuts in standard preparation. Some home-style versions add cream or ghee. High purine content — flag for gout.
north_indian.alternatives: Jain version without onion/garlic. Confirm no cream added. Good for dairy-free and nut-free diets.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Paneer IS dairy. Cooked in shared tandoor with naan.
north_indian.alternatives: Mushroom tikka or vegetable seekh kebab — but confirm yogurt-free preparation.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Butter is not just an ingredient — it is THE ingredient. Pav is a wheat bread roll. The bhaji itself may be wheat-free but is cooked on a butter-laden griddle.
north_indian.alternatives: Eat the bhaji only (without pav) for wheat-free — but dairy remains. No realistic dairy-free version.

north_indian.hidden_risks: One of the safest options in Indian cuisine. Naturally dairy-free, nut-free, egg-free, wheat-free. Fermented — may trigger histamine sensitivity. Served with sambar (may contain allium) and coconut chutney (tree nut debate).
north_indian.alternatives: Eat plain or with tomato chutney to avoid coconut and allium.

north_indian.hidden_risks: Plain dosa is safe. Masala dosa filling contains potato + onion + mustard seeds. Some restaurants spread ghee on the dosa. Served with sambar and coconut chutney — same concerns as idli.
north_indian.alternatives: Specify 'plain dosa without ghee.' Fermented — histamine concern.

north_indian.hidden_risks: One of the few North Indian-adjacent dishes that is typically dairy-free and nut-free (Goan Portuguese influence). Very heavy on garlic. Extremely spicy.
north_indian.alternatives: Good option for dairy/nut-free diets if allium is not an issue.

japanese.hidden_risks: Dashi is invisible but present. 'Vegetarian' miso soup in Japan is rare — assume fish unless confirmed. Barley miso (mugi miso) contains gluten.
japanese.alternatives: Request kombu-only dashi (no bonito) — some places can accommodate. Avoid entirely if soy allergy.

japanese.hidden_risks: Sushi rice contains rice vinegar, sugar, and salt — generally safe. Cross-contact on cutting boards between different fish and shellfish. Wasabi may be horseradish-based imitation. Conveyor belt sushi has high cross-contact risk.
japanese.alternatives: Kappa maki (cucumber roll) or tamago nigiri for fish-free. Eat without soy sauce for wheat/soy-free.

japanese.hidden_risks: Even vegetable tempura is fried in the same oil as shrimp tempura. Tentsuyu dipping sauce contains dashi (fish), soy sauce (wheat), and mirin. All tempura is a cross-contact minefield.
japanese.alternatives: No safe tempura variant for wheat, egg, or shellfish allergies. Choose grilled (yaki) items instead.

egg.source: ajitsuke tamago (marinated egg), some noodles contain egg

fish.source: fish-based broth in some varieties, bonito in tare

japanese.hidden_risks: Ramen is a multi-allergen dish. Tonkotsu broth is pork. Shoyu ramen tare is soy+wheat. Even 'shio' (salt) ramen typically contains dashi. Egg noodles are standard.
japanese.alternatives: No safe ramen for wheat allergy. Rice noodle substitution is not standard in Japan. Udon has the same wheat issue.

wheat.source: most soba is 60-80% buckwheat + wheat flour

japanese.hidden_risks: Buckwheat allergy can cause severe anaphylaxis. Even '100% buckwheat' (juwari soba) may be processed on shared equipment with wheat. Soba cooking water (sobayu) is served as a drink — buckwheat exposure. Dipping sauce contains soy sauce (wheat) and dashi (fish).
japanese.alternatives: Udon if buckwheat is the concern (but contains wheat). Rice dishes if both are concerns.

japanese.hidden_risks: Triple wheat exposure (flour, egg wash, panko). Tonkatsu sauce contains soy sauce. Shared fryer with other breaded items. Katsu curry adds curry roux (also contains wheat flour).
japanese.alternatives: Grilled pork (buta shogayaki — ginger pork) avoids breading but contains soy sauce.

japanese.hidden_risks: This IS soy. Otherwise very simple and free of other common allergens.
japanese.alternatives: Salted cucumber (tsukemono) as a simple starter alternative.

soy.source: soy sauce in filling and for dipping

japanese.hidden_risks: Sesame oil is standard in the filling but rarely mentioned. Dipping sauce is soy-based. Pan-fried in shared oil.
japanese.alternatives: No standard wheat-free gyoza in Japan.

ingredients: filling (varies: salmon, umeboshi, tuna mayo, kombu)

soy.source: soy sauce seasoning in some fillings

japanese.hidden_risks: Filling determines allergen profile. Umeboshi (pickled plum) and kombu (kelp) are the safest fillings. Convenience store onigiri have ingredient lists in Japanese.
japanese.alternatives: Choose umeboshi or kombu filling for fish/egg/dairy-free. One of the safest grab-and-go options in Japan.

japanese.hidden_risks: Udon cooking water is often shared with soba — buckwheat cross-contact. Toppings like tempura add egg, wheat, shellfish exposure.
japanese.alternatives: No wheat-free udon. Rice dishes or sashimi without soy sauce for wheat-free meals.

japanese.hidden_risks: Order 'shio' (salt) style to avoid tare sauce — this eliminates soy and wheat. Shared grill surface between tare and shio items.
japanese.alternatives: Shio yakitori is one of the safest Japanese options for soy/wheat-free diets.

japanese.hidden_risks: This IS egg. Dashi adds hidden fish. Common sushi topping and bento item.

fish.source: dashi in batter, bonito flakes topping

japanese.hidden_risks: Multi-allergen dish. Shared griddle with other orders. Nagaimo (mountain yam) is added to batter in some styles. Kewpie mayo contains egg.
japanese.alternatives: No safe version for wheat or egg allergies.

ingredients: curry roux (wheat flour, oil, curry powder)
japanese.hidden_risks: The roux block is the hidden wheat source. Store-bought roux (S&B, House) contains wheat, soy, and often dairy. Apple and honey are also common additions.
japanese.alternatives: No standard wheat-free Japanese curry in restaurants. Indian curry over rice is safer for wheat-free.

shellfish.source: if platter includes shrimp, squid, or shellfish

japanese.hidden_risks: Sashimi itself is simple, but soy sauce for dipping contains wheat. Cross-contact on cutting boards. Served on shared platters at izakayas.
japanese.alternatives: One of the simpler Japanese dishes if fish is not a concern. Skip soy sauce for wheat-free.

hindi.allergy_alert: मुझे [ALLERGEN] से गंभीर एलर्जी है। अगर मैं यह खा लूँ तो मेरी जान को खतरा हो सकता है। यह पसंद नहीं, मेडिकल इमरजेंसी है। कृपया किचन से पक्का कर लीजिए।
hindi.allergy_alert_transliteration: Mujhe [ALLERGEN] se gambhir allergy hai. Agar main yeh kha loon to meri jaan ko khatra ho sakta hai. Yeh pasand nahi, medical emergency hai. Kripya kitchen se pakka kar lijiye.
hindi.allergy_alert_english: I have a severe allergy to [ALLERGEN]. If I eat this, my life could be in danger. This is not a preference — it is a medical emergency. Please confirm with the kitchen.
hindi.emergency: Mujhe allergy ka reaction ho raha hai. Mujhe hospital le jaiye. Emergency number: 112

japanese.allergy_alert: [ALLERGEN]の重度のアレルギーがあります。これを食べると命に関わります。好き嫌いではなく、医療上の緊急事態です。厨房に確認してください。
japanese.allergy_alert_transliteration: [ALLERGEN] no juudo no arerugii ga arimasu. Kore wo taberu to inochi ni kakawarimasu. Sukikirai dewa naku, iryoujou no kinkyuu jitai desu. Chuubou ni kakunin shite kudasai.
japanese.allergy_alert_english: I have a severe allergy to [ALLERGEN]. If I eat this, my life could be in danger. This is not a preference — it is a medical emergency. Please confirm with the kitchen.
japanese.emergency: Arerugii no hannō ga okite imasu. Kyūkyūsha wo yonde kudasai. Emergency number: 119

celiac.note: In Japanese cuisine, tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) is the alternative. Most regular soy sauce contains wheat.

ckd.note: Many Indian dishes are high in potassium (dal, potato dishes) and phosphorus (dairy, nuts).

diabetes.note: Fried rice and curry over rice are high glycemic.

gout.note: Rajma (kidney beans), dal, and seafood-heavy Japanese dishes are high purine.

limit: fermented foods (miso, soy sauce, fish sauce, kimchi)
histamine_intolerance.note: Japanese cuisine is extremely high-histamine (miso, soy sauce, dashi, pickles). Indian fermented items (idli, dosa, pickles) are triggers.

fodmap.note: North Indian cuisine is extremely challenging for low-FODMAP — onion and garlic are in nearly everything. Jain cooking (no onion/garlic) is the closest safe option.
